name: Vijnanabhikshu

lineage_polemical.note: Vijñānabhikṣu's Yoga-Vārttika is the principal late-medieval re-engagement with the Pātañjala-Yoga-Śāstra from a Vedāntic harmonising standpoint; the relation is constructive on praxis and on the absorption of Yoga into avibhāga-advaita, polemical on the standalone reading of īśvara as merely a special puruṣa.

core_thesis:
Vijñānabhikṣu's project is the reconciliation of Sāṃkhya, Yoga, and Vedānta within a single hierarchical ontology (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya*, upodghāta and on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.2](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.2)). The signature concept is *avibhāga* — non-separation — and the technical name for the position is *avibhāga-advaita* or, in some doxographies, *avibhāgādvaita* (so codified by later doxographers; Bhikṣu's own programmatic statement is at *Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.1.22](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.1.22) and 2.3.43). The thesis is precise.

During manifestation (*sṛṣṭi*), Brahman, *cit*, *acit*, and the plural *jīvas* are really differentiated (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.43), on *aṃśo nānāvyapadeśāt*). The differences are not appearance, and Sāṃkhya's analytic apparatus — the *tattvas*, the *prakṛti–puruṣa* distinction, the plurality of *puruṣas* — is taken as a substantively accurate description of the manifest order, true within its domain (*Sāṃkhya-Pravacana-Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Sūtra* 1.61–67, defending the analytic structure). At *pralaya* and at *mokṣa* the differences are really resolved into non-separation: not into identity-from-eternity in Śaṅkara's sense (cf. *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14), but into *avibhāga* — a structural non-separateness that is genuinely achieved when the cycle returns (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 4.4.1–4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/4.4.1-4)). The crucial distinction here is between *abheda* as never-having-been-different and *avibhāga* as not-separated-now (drawn explicitly at [*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* 2.1.22](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.1.22)). Vijñānabhikṣu argues for the second and refuses the first.

The synthesis is hierarchical, not eclectic. Sāṃkhya's *tattvas* and Yoga's *sādhana* — particularly *citta-vṛtti-nirodha* ([*Yoga-Sūtra* 1.2](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.2) with Bhikṣu's *Yoga-Vārttika*) and *samādhi* (*Yoga-Vārttika* on [*Yoga-Sūtra* 1.23](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.23)) — are read as preparatory and partially valid stages within an ontology crowned by Brahman. Theistic Vedānta reads as fuller. The *Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* mounts a head-on critique of post-Śaṅkara *māyā-vāda* (notably at *Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.4) and 1.4.3), which Vijñānabhikṣu diagnoses as a deviation from genuine Vedānta and as *pracchanna-bauddha* — repeating, with his own emphasis, Bhāskara's earlier charge (*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya of Bhāskara* on 1.1.4). The non-dualism is not abandoned in the critique. It is relocated: from identity-from-eternity to non-separation-as-limit-state ([*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* 4.4.1–4](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/4.4.1-4)).

The argumentative structure deserves attention on its own terms. Vijñānabhikṣu is not arguing that the māyāvādin's conclusion is wrong because non-dualism is wrong; he is arguing that the māyāvādin's specific way of securing non-dualism — by reducing the world's reality — is unnecessary and produces incoherences a hierarchical *avibhāga* ontology avoids (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.4–5](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.4-5), where *māyā* is identified with *prakṛti* rather than with epistemic *avidyā*). The position is, in this sense, an internal Vedāntic revision rather than an anti-Vedāntic polemic.

engaged_works.title: Vijnanamrta-Bhashya on Brahma-Sutra
engaged_works.summary: The *Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* is Vijñānabhikṣu's commentary on the *Brahma-Sūtras* and the school-defining text of *avibhāga-advaita*. The bhāṣya construes the *sūtras* as licensing a hierarchical ontology: Sāṃkhya's *prakṛti–puruṣa* analysis is preparatory and partially valid (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.1–7](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.4.1-7), on the *ānumānika* adhikaraṇa), theistic Vedānta is fuller (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* upodghāta), and the *avibhāga* of Brahman, *cit*, and *acit* at *pralaya* and *mokṣa* is the limit-state (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 4.4.1–4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/4.4.1-4)). The polemical engagement is sustained and, in its specific argumentative shape, important. Vijñānabhikṣu mounts a head-on critique of post-Śaṅkara *māyā-vāda* (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.4–5](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.4-5)). He diagnoses it as a deviation from genuine Vedānta and as *pracchanna-bauddha* — repeating Bhāskara's charge (*Bhāskara-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.4)) while developing it on his own terms — and he distinguishes his own non-dualism from Śaṅkara's by treating *avibhāga* as an achieved limit-state rather than as identity-from-eternity ([*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* 2.1.22](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.1.22) contrasted with Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14). The argument-structure matters: the critique of *māyā-vāda* is internal to a defense of non-dualism, not a defection from it (so the *upodghāta*, where Bhikṣu programmatically affirms *advaita* while rejecting *vivarta*). He also engages Rāmānuja and the *bhedābheda* lines (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.43), on the *jīva* as *aṃśa*), reading their positions as partial truths absorbed into the hierarchical synthesis. The bhāṣya is the principal source for *avibhāga-advaita* and the most ambitious early-modern Vedānta–Sāṃkhya–Yoga reconciliation (so Dasgupta, *History of Indian Philosophy*, vol. III, ch. on Bhikṣu).

engaged_works.title: Yoga-Varttika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored vārttika on the Yoga-Sūtras.
engaged_works.summary: The *Yoga-Vārttika* is Vijñānabhikṣu's gloss on Vyāsa's *Yoga-Bhāṣya* on Patañjali's *Yoga-Sūtras*, paired with his shorter *Yoga-Sāra-Saṅgraha*. The works treat Patañjali's *Yoga-Darśana* as a serious *darśana* with its own technical contribution — particularly on *citta-vṛtti-nirodha* (*Yoga-Vārttika* on [*Yoga-Sūtra* 1.2](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.2)), *samādhi* (*Yoga-Vārttika* on [*Yoga-Sūtra* 1.17–23](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.17-23)), and *kaivalya* (*Yoga-Vārttika* on [*Yoga-Sūtra* 4.34](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.34)) — while reading the Yoga material within the hierarchical ontology developed in the *Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya*. Vijñānabhikṣu defends the theistic reading of Patañjali. *Īśvara* in [*Yoga-Sūtra* 1.23–24](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.23-24) is a special *puruṣa* — untouched by *kleśa*, *karma*, *vipāka*, *āśaya* (*Yoga-Vārttika* on [*Yoga-Sūtra* 1.24](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.24)) — and is contemplated in *Īśvara-praṇidhāna* (*Yoga-Vārttika* on [*Yoga-Sūtra* 1.23](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.23)). The Yoga corpus is therefore used to demonstrate that classical *darśanas* are stratified levels of one truth, with Vedānta crowning the hierarchy and Yoga supplying technically precise *sādhana* (*Yoga-Sāra-Saṅgraha*, opening *upodghāta*). The technical engagement with *kaivalya* in particular allows Vijñānabhikṣu to clarify what *avibhāga* is and is not. *Kaivalya* in classical Sāṃkhya-Yoga is *puruṣa*'s isolation from *prakṛti* ([*Yoga-Sūtra* 4.34](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.34)); *avibhāga* in Vijñānabhikṣu's reading is non-separation in a stronger ontological sense, in which the resolved *puruṣas* are not just isolated from *prakṛti* but non-separated within Brahman (*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 4.4.1–4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/4.4.1-4), read with [*Yoga-Vārttika* 4.34](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/4.34)). The works are major early-modern contributions to the integrated Sāṃkhya–Yoga–Vedānta reading that defines Vijñānabhikṣu's project (Larson and Bhattacharya, eds., *Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies*, Vol. XII: Yoga, intro. and Bhikṣu sections).

engaged_works.title: Samkhya-Pravacana-Bhashya
engaged_works.summary: The *Sāṃkhya-Pravacana-Bhāṣya* is Vijñānabhikṣu's commentary on the *Sāṃkhya-Sūtras*. Alongside Gauḍapāda's *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā-Bhāṣya* and Vācaspati Miśra's *Tattva-Kaumudī*, it is the most influential pre-modern Sāṃkhya commentary (Larson, *Classical Sāṃkhya*, 1979, ch. 4). Two doctrinal moves give the work its significance. First, the theistic reading of Sāṃkhya: Vijñānabhikṣu defends a personal *Īśvara* as compatible with classical Sāṃkhya's *prakṛti–puruṣa* analysis (*Sāṃkhya-Pravacana-Bhāṣya* on *Sāṃkhya-Sūtra* 1.92–94, 3.56–57), against the dominant a-theistic interpretation associated with the *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* tradition. The argument is that the standard arguments for Sāṃkhya's atheism turn on textual omissions rather than on positive incompatibility, and that *Īśvara* can be admitted without compromising the *prakṛti–puruṣa* dualism within the manifest order ([*Sāṃkhya-Pravacana-Bhāṣya* on 1.92](cite://vijnanabhiksu/samkhya-pravacana-bhasya/1.92), the *na-īśvara-siddhiḥ* sūtra read constructively). Second, the integration of Sāṃkhya into the wider hierarchical ontology of *avibhāga-advaita*: the classical Sāṃkhya plurality of *puruṣas* and the dualism of *puruṣa* and *prakṛti* are read as accurate descriptions of the manifest order, true within their domain, and resolved at *pralaya* into the non-separation that *avibhāga-advaita* identifies as the highest truth ([*Sāṃkhya-Pravacana-Bhāṣya* on 1.18–19, 6.59](cite://vijnanabhiksu/samkhya-pravacana-bhasya/1.18-19, 6.59), read with [*Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya* 4.4.1–4](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/4.4.1-4)). The bhāṣya is also a major source on classical Sāṃkhya doctrine in its own right and the standard reference for the post-classical Sāṃkhya–Vedānta synthesis (Larson, *Classical Sāṃkhya*, 1979; Dasgupta, *History of Indian Philosophy*, vol. III).

key_passages.locus_long: Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya on Brahma-Sūtra 2.3.43, on aṃśo nānāvyapadeśāt
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
aṃśo nānāvyapadeśād anyathā cāpi dāsakitavāditvam adhīyate eke //
nityatvādinā sajātīyatvasyopapāditatayā jīvo brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ, pitur iva putraḥ //

pada_analysis.morphology: abl sg masc (surface sandhi form of nānāvyapadeśāt)

pada_analysis.gloss: the condition of being spoken of as servant, gambler, and the like

samasa_vigrahas.note: predication in many forms or as many

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: dāsaś ca kitavaś cādīni ca; teṣāṃ bhāvaḥ
samasa_vigrahas.note: the state of being designated as servant, gambler, and so on

karaka_structure.note: logical agent of the passive adhīyate
what is taught in some recensions
genitive relation with aṃśaḥ in the nominal predication

key_passages.english_close: There is a part, because multiplicity is predicated, and because the condition of being servant, gambler, and so on is also taught by some. Since common nature is established by eternity and the like, the jīva is a part of Brahman, like a son of a father.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: part, or derivative share that remains linked to its source
common nature or sameness of kind

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the place where Bhikṣu directly derives the jīva–Brahman relation from the Brahma-Sūtra as a genuine aṃśa–aṃśin relation. It fairly represents his ontology because it excludes both sheer identity and sheer separateness in favor of a structured non-separation with real differentiation.
key_passages.source_edition: Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya, Sanskrit edition (1979), ed. Kedāranātha Tripāṭhī; archive.org item vijnanamrtabhasya_1979; transcribed against materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/vijnanabhiksu_vijnanamrta_bhasya_1979_archive.pdf.

key_passages.locus_long: Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya on Brahma-Sūtra 1.1.5, on īkṣater nāśabdam
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tasmāt “māyāṃ tu prakṛtiṃ vidyān māyinaṃ tu maheśvaram /
asyāvayavabhūtais tu vyāptaṃ sarvam idaṃ jagat //”
iti śrutau śrutyantara-smṛti-prasiddha-prakṛter eva māyāśabda-vācyatvaṃ vidhīyate iti.

pada_analysis.gloss: by what have become its constituent parts
of the prakṛti well known from other śruti and smṛti

samasa_vigrahas.note: those that have become constituent parts of it
prakṛti known from other scriptural sources
status of being denoted by the term māyā

karaka_structure.note: second accusative in the quoted injunction
locus in which the prescription is found

key_passages.english_close: Therefore, in the scriptural statement: 'One should know māyā to be prakṛti, and the possessor of māyā to be Maheśvara'. [And further]: 'by what [parts] have become its constituent parts, this whole world is pervaded'. In this śruti, it is prescribed that it is precisely the prakṛti — already known from other śruti and smṛti — that is denoted by the word 'māyā'.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: primordial material matrix, the transforming causal power
the possessor or wielder of māyā

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a representative doctrinal passage because Bhikṣu here explicitly identifies māyā with prakṛti rather than treating it as a purely epistemic avidyā. That move is central to his synthesis of Vedānta with Sāṃkhya-Yoga and to his account of how Brahman, Lord, and cosmological causation fit together.
key_passages.source_edition: Vijñānāmṛta-Bhāṣya, Sanskrit edition (1979), ed. Kedāranātha Tripāṭhī; archive.org item vijnanamrtabhasya_1979; transcribed against materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/vijnanabhiksu_vijnanamrta_bhasya_1979_archive.pdf.

key_passages.locus_long: Yoga-Vārttika on Yoga-Sūtra 1.23, on īśvara-praṇidhānād vā
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
īśvara-praṇidhānād vā //
praṇidhānam atra na dvitīya-pādavad japyaprāyaṃ, kiṃ tv asaṃprajñāta-kāraṇībhūta-samādhi-bhāvanā-viśeṣam eva //

pada_analysis.morphology: abl sg neut (surface sandhi form of īśvara-praṇidhānāt)
pada_analysis.gloss: from devotion-directing of oneself to the Lord
a particular cultivation of samādhi that becomes the cause of asaṃprajñāta

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: asaṃprajñātasya kāraṇībhūtā samādheḥ bhāvanā; tasyā viśeṣaḥ
samasa_vigrahas.note: a specialized contemplation productive of the higher samādhi

key_passages.english_close: Or from devotion-directing oneself to the Lord. Here praṇidhāna is not, as in the second pāda, something consisting chiefly in muttered recitation; rather it is precisely a particular cultivation of samādhi that becomes the cause of asaṃprajñāta [samādhi].

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: deliberate placing of oneself toward an object of total commitment
the non-discursive or seedless form of samādhi
absorptive concentration in which the mind is brought to one-pointed stillness

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a fair selection because Bhikṣu here states exactly how he reads a classical Yoga sūtra within his larger synthesis: īśvara-praṇidhāna is a serious contemplative means to the highest samādhi, not a merely external devotional add-on. The passage captures his effort to keep Yoga technically rigorous while still explicitly theistic.
key_passages.source_edition: Yoga-Vārttika, 1884 Sanskrit edition prepared by Rāmakṛṣṇaśāstrī Paṭavardhana and Keśavaśāstrī; archive.org item VijnanabhiksuYogavarttika1884; transcribed against materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/vijnanabhiksu_yoga_varttika_1884_archive.pdf.

key_passages.locus_long: Yoga-Vārttika on Yoga-Sūtra 1.24, on kleśa-karma-vipākāśayair aparāmṛṣṭaḥ
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
kleśa-karma-vipākāśayair aparāmṛṣṭaḥ puruṣa-viśeṣa īśvaraḥ //
avidyā-asmitā-rāga-dveṣābhiniveśāḥ kleśā iti vakṣyate //

pada_analysis.gloss: by afflictions, actions, ripenings, and latent deposits
ignorance, egoity, attachment, aversion, and clinging-to-life

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: kleśāś ca karmāṇi ca vipākāśayāś ca
samasa_vigrahas.note: a chain of the bondage-producing factors
a distinct class or type of puruṣa

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: avidyā cāsmitā ca rāgaś ca dveṣaś cābhiniveśaś ca

karaka_structure.note: instrumental of that by which Īśvara is not touched
predicate content later to be defined by vakṣyate

verb_modality.note: future sense is contextual; formally present passive in commentarial idiom

key_passages.english_close: Īśvara is a special puruṣa untouched by afflictions, actions, their ripenings, and latent deposits. 'Afflictions' are said to be ignorance, egoity, attachment, aversion, and clinging-to-life.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: afflictive factor that binds cognition and action

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is representative because Bhikṣu's theistic Yoga depends on reading Īśvara as neither reducible to ordinary bound selves nor detachable from the puruṣa framework inherited from Sāṃkhya-Yoga. The passage gives his compact doctrinal definition of the personal Lord and immediately anchors it in the standard kleśa-analysis.
key_passages.source_edition: Yoga-Vārttika, 1884 Sanskrit edition prepared by Rāmakṛṣṇaśāstrī Paṭavardhana and Keśavaśāstrī; archive.org item VijnanabhiksuYogavarttika1884; transcribed against materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/vijnanabhiksu_yoga_varttika_1884_archive.pdf.
